coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor based on F,N-doped carbon dots decorated laccase for detection of catechol
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Li
Anwar, Sadat
Ding, Haizhen
Xu, Mingsheng
Yin, Qiang
Xiao, Yazhong
Yang, Xingyuan
Yan, Manqing
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of carbon nanomaterial have gained tremendous attention due to their wide applications in photocatalyst, sensor, bioimaging and so on. In the present work, a novel kind of fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs (F,N-CDs) have been synthesized from 5-fluorouracil (5-Fu) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) via a hydrothermal method. An electrochemical sensor for detecting catechol was designed and constructed on the basis of F,N-CDs and laccase (Lac). Compared with the traditional detection methods of catechol, the as-fabricated sensor Lac-F,N-CDs/glassy carbon electrode (GCE) shows a comparatively low detection limit (0.014μM) as well as a high sensitivity (219.17μAcm−2 mΜ−1) through the amperometric i-t responses method. The most significant feature of the sensor is its long cycle stability and can be used for many times (≥50) under ambient conditions. The prepared Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor has been employed for detection of catechol in tap water and lake water successfully. This work provides a new pathway to stabilize laccase by using CDs and other carbon nanomaterials to construct efficient electrochemical sensor for detecting catechol and its analogue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Laccase
Electrochemical sensor
Catechol
Fluorine and nitrogen doping

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) A typical TEM image of F,N-CDs (inset: the lateral size distribution); (B) HRTEM image of an individual F,N-CD; (C) TEM image of Lac-F,N-CDs complex; (D–I) Fluorescence microscope images of F,N-CDs (D, E, F) and Lac-F,N-CDs complex (G, H, I). The images were obtained under the excitation wavelength of 405, 448 and 550nm from left to right.
XPS spectra of the F,N-CDs: (A) the survey spectrum; (B) the high-resolution C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) F1s spectra, respectively.

(A) UV–Vis absorbance and PL emission spectra of F,N-CDs aqueous solution (the inset shows the picture of F,N-CDs under visible light and 365nm UV lamp); (B) UV–Vis absorbance of different concentrations of F,N-CDs and laccase complex; (C) PL emission spectra of Lac-F,N-CDs complex (150μgmL−1); (D) Fluorescence responses of F,N-CDs (0.68mgmL−1) to different metal ions (0.88mM), respectively.

(A) CV responses of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE to catechol (1.0mM) obtained at different scan rates from 10 to 250mVs−1; (B) The linear plot of peak current density vs. scan rates; (C) CV curves of 50 times cycle measured on the same day of preparation; (D) CV curves obtained using different electrodes: bare GCE, F,N-CDs/GCE and Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE in absence (−) and presence (+) of catechol (scan rates from 100mVs−1).

(A) Amperometric i-t responses and (B) the corresponding magnified region in 0–600s; (C) the linear plot for the amperometric current response of the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE to different concentration additions (0.1 to 0.45mM) of catechol into the constantly stirred acetate electrolyte, working potential=0.6V; Amperometric i-t response of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensors to (D) different substrates of the same concentration and (E) the test when interfering substance concentration is 10 times the catechol.

The CV curves of (A) the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE to catechol, hydroquinone, dopamine and gallic acid in catechol solutions and (B) the Lac/GCE and Lac-FN-CDs/GCE are measured in catechol solutions (scan rate is 100mVs−1).
A comparison of our work with the reported electrochemical sensors for detection of catechol in the literature.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * The saturated calomel electrode was used as reference electrode and the platinum electrode as the counter electrode.

* SPE (screen printed electrode); Tyr (tyrosinase);OMC-Au (ordered mesoporous carbon-Au); PSS (poly (sodium 4-styrenesulfonate)); MWCNTs (multi-walled Carbon nanotubes); GR-CMF (graphene-cellulose microfibers); FYSSns (flower-shaped yolk-shell SiO2 nanospheres); GNRs (graphene nanoribbons); PPO (polyphenol oxidase).

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor based on F,N-doped carbon dots decorated laccase for detection of catechol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Faculty of Health Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of carbon nanomaterial have gained tremendous attention due to their wide applications in photocatalyst, sensor, bioimaging and so on. In the present work, a novel kind of fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs (F,N-CDs) have been synthesized from 5-fluorouracil (5-Fu) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) via a hydrothermal method. An electrochemical sensor for detecting catechol was designed and constructed on the basis of F,N-CDs and laccase (Lac). Compared with the traditional detection methods of catechol, the as-fabricated sensor Lac-F,N-CDs/glassy carbon electrode (GCE) shows a comparatively low detection limit (0.014μM) as well as a high sensitivity (219.17μAcm−2 mΜ−1) through the amperometric i-t responses method. The most significant feature of the sensor is its long cycle stability and can be used for many times (≥50) under ambient conditions. The prepared Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor has been employed for detection of catechol in tap water and lake water successfully. This work provides a new pathway to stabilize laccase by using CDs and other carbon nanomaterials to construct efficient electrochemical sensor for detecting catechol and its analogue.

An electrochemical sensor for detecting catechol was designed and constructed on the basis of F,N-doped carbon dots (F,N-CDs) and laccase (Lac). Compared with the traditional detection methods of catechol, the as-fabricated sensor Lac-F,N-CDs/glassy carbon electrode (GCE) shows a comparatively low detection limit (0.014μM), a high sensitivity (219.17μAcm−2 mΜ−1) and a good cycle stability through the amperometric i-t responses method.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: In nowadays, catechol and other phenol compounds have a serious hazard to environment and human health. The major sources of these toxic substances derive from various kinds of agricultural, chemical and industrial byproducts [1–3]. Generally, catechol as a representative of phenol compounds can be detected by many techniques such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fluorescent probes [4–6] and electrochemical sensors [7–12]. In particular, electrochemical sensor is based on enzymatic redox reaction which produces or consumes electrons to catalyze the reaction under given conditions, and thus this technique is more advantageous owing to low cost, high sensitivity, reproducibility and fast response [10,13,14].

Immobilization of enzyme on the surface of materials is a versatile method to synthesize and construct efficient biomaterials for sensors, particularly for the detection of the catechol in water and food samples. Previously, a laccase-based biosensor based was based on MoS2 and graphene quantum dots as electrode modifiers for sensitive detection of polyphenols [15]. Vlamidis Y. et al. studied tyrosinase and laccase based biosensors, and used catechol as a substrate to optimize the performance of the biosensor [16]. Ormategui N et al. prepared a highly stable biocatalyst by immobilizing laccase on a polymer/graphene hydrogel carrier [17]. Recently, screen printed electrodes were modified with carbon nanotubes/gold nanoparticles followed by covalent bonding of Tyr [10]. Mohtar L.G. et al. synthesized a laccase-based nanocomposite to construct an electrochemical biosensor that can be used to detect polyphenols [18]. Both tyrosinase and laccase are copper-containing enzymes. However, laccase is a protein comprised of two or three beta-barrel domains containing four copper ions, in which there are one type 1 (T1), one type 2 (T2) and two type 3 copper (T3) active centers. Compared with tyrosinase-based sensors, laccase has a higher ability to catalyze bioelectrochemical redox reactions [19–20]. In the recent years, it is of great interest to immobilize laccase on various nanomaterials. Many studies have indicated that laccase can degrade a variety of pollutants due to broad substrate specificity and catalytic activity especially for the phenolic pollutants. Unfortunately, free enzyme cannot be used alone because of the poor stability under ambient conditions [1]. More interestingly, the catalytic activity of laccase might increase under certain conditions if laccase is combined with specific nanomaterials [21–22]. Therefore, there is a demand to develop new methods for the stabilization of laccase under the ambient conditions and to prevent the loss as well as to ensure the cycling stability.

The carbon dots (CDs) are one of most auspicious and newly divulged materials to be employed in photocatalyst, sensor, bioimaging and so on due to their fascinating merits such as excellent optical properties, high resistance to photobleaching, prominent physical and chemical stability, low toxicity, ease of handling and environmental amiability [23–26]. Furthermore, surface modification and functionalization are used to modulate the fluorescent properties of CDs and to ameliorate the reactivity and selectivity of the materials, so that the CDs can be fabricated as an efficient probe for biosensor or bioimaging [27–29]. For examples, Li, H et al. reported that the activity of the complex of CDs and porcine pancreatic lipase was enhanced or inhibited by the free enzyme in the presence or absence of visible light irradiation [21]. Very recently, Tang et al. constructed a CDs-tyrosinase bioconjugate fluorescent probe for detecting dopamine, on the basis of the induced fluorescence quenching by an efficient electron transfer from the excited CDs to dopaquinone [30]. Besides, various metal ions and heteroatoms are doped into CDs to enhance the optical, chemical and biocompatible properties, and thus the doped CDs may be more promising materials for biomedical applications such as sensor, bioimaging and nanomedicine [27,31,32]. In particular, it has attracted considerable interest to dope CDs with dual heteroatoms, among which nitrogen (N) is one of the most widely used elements in preparation of heteroatoms-doped CDs [33].

Nitrogen doped CDs have N-related defects which exhibited noticeable electron donating/accepting potentiality [34]. As previously reported, the incorporated nitrogen could provide electron donor states in N-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes (CNx-MWCNT), which conferred more sensitivity to CNx-MWCNT than to graphene oxide [8]. In addition, the N atoms may act as an electron donor when N-doped CDs coupled with Fe3+ ions [35] and horseradish peroxidase containing Fe3+ active center [36]. Besides, fluoride is a semi-essential element of the human body, and fluoride protects people's teeth from caries and enhances mineral deposits in bones [37], and the fluorous ligands are generally bioinert, which is beneficial for avoiding protein denaturation [38]. F∙ as an electron-withdrawing group, F-doping was reported to be able to improve the photostability and biocompatibility of CDs [39–40]. Doping of F and N in carbon dots is beneficial to improve their electrochemical properties and enhance the stability of enzymes and carbon dots complexes.

In the present work, a novel kind of fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs (F,N-CDs) have been synthesized from 5-fluorouracil (5-Fu) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) via a hydrothermal carbonization method. The as-synthesized F,N-CDs can form a complex with laccase, named as Lac-F,N-CDs. Lac-F,N-CDs was deposited on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to construct a new Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor for the electrochemical detection of catechol. The Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor shows remarkable stability and can efficiently detect/differentiate catechol and its analogue in the samples. The most significant features of the Lac-F,N-CDs complex are its long cycle stability and a high sensitivity in detecting catechol by chronoamperometry. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time we use complex of laccase with F and N co-doped CDs for detecting catechol and it has been successfully achieved. Electrochemical sensor based on Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE have potential applications for detecting catechol content in tap water and lake water, sea food and compost bioremediation, etc. This work provides a new approach to construct electrochemical biosensors based on the composites of non-metal doped CDs and enzyme for the detection of catechol and its analogues.

All purchased chemicals were used without further purification. p-PD was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. 5-Fu was obtained from Energy Chemical. Oleic acid was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Corporation. Catechol, glucose, hydroquinone, dopamine, gallic acid, phenol, p-nitrophenol, dibasic sodium phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, acetic acid, sodium acetate, BiCl3, FeCl2·4H2O, CaCl2, SnCl4·5H2O, NaCl, MnCl2·4H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, FeCl3, CuCl, CuCl2 and FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Laccase was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Corporation. The water used in the experiment is ultrapure water produced by the Millipore system.

F,N-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using p-PD and 5-Fu. Briefly, p-PD (0.195g, 1.8mmol) and 5-Fu (0.234g, 1.8mmol) were separately dissolved in 15mL ultrapure water followed by mixing the two reactants under sonication. The mixture was then fetched into a 50mL stainless steel autoclave with Teflon lining and stowed in a furnace maintaining the reaction temperature at 200°C for 2h followed by cooling down to room temperature. A purple brownish color solution was obtained by removing insoluble particles through negative-pressure filtration. The filtrate was then shaken for 5min with oleic acid, impurities and unreacted starting materials were extracted into oleic acid phase, and the F,N-CDs were left-behind in the aqueous phase which yielded 200mg of F,N-CDs powder upon lyophilization for 48h.

Lac-F,N-CDs complex was synthesized by dissolving 10mg of laccase in 20mL of phosphate buffer solution (PB, pH=6), and stirred at 4°C for 24h and subsequently centrifuged at 2000rpm for 20min×3 to remove the insoluble substances. Next, an aqueous solution of F,N-CDs at concentrations of 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 200 and 250μgmL−1 was prepared. Last, 0.2mL of each was added to 2mL of laccase solution (500μgmL−1), and then stirred at 4°C for 24h to obtain a series of concentrations of the Lac-F,N-CDs complex.
The 10μL Lac-F,N-CDs complex solution was dripped on the surface of a polished glassy carbon electrode and dried at 4°C to obtain a Lac-F,N-CDs based sensor (Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE).

Quantum yield (QY) of F,N-CDs was determined by reference method [5]. The quinoline sulfate salt (solvent: 0.01mM sulfuric acid solution) was selected as a standard reference (QY 54%), and the FN-CDs was dissolved in ultrapure water. The integrated fluorescence intensities of the two dilute solutions of FN-CDs and the reference substance are from Fluorescence Spectroscopy, and the absorbance of incident light at the same excitation wavelength (λ ex =360nm) are from UV–Vis spectrum. Then, by substituting these values into a specific formula for calculation, the quantum yield of the FN-CDs can be obtained. The calculation formula is Eq. (1):
(1)
φ sam
=
φ ref
·
I sam
I ref
·
A ref
A sam
·
η sam 2
η ref 2
where, φ represents the quantum yield, I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity; A represents the absorbance. When using this formula, the absorbance A are generally required to be <0.05. η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts sam and ref represent two test samples for FN-CDs and standard reference, respectively.

Morphology and composition of the F,N-CDs microstructure were determined by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan). The surface properties were characterized by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250, USA). Surface functional groups of F,N-CDs were detected by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Vertex80+Hyperion2000, China) and the nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR, AVWBlll600, Germany). Absorbance and fluorescent properties were determined by the fluorescence (FL, F-7000) spectrophotometer and the ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were studied on the confocal laser microraman spectrometer (Raman, inVia-Reflex, England), equipped with a 325nm laser. The absolute quantum yield of samples was detected by the full-function steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLSP920, China). Fluorescence images were taken by the fluorescence microscopy (CX51, Japan). The particle size analysis of the sample in aqueous solution was carried out by the visual multispectral particle analyzer (ViewSizer 3000, USA). The content of catechol in the actual water sample was tested by the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Delta600, China).

All electrochemical measurements were carried out in an electrolyte of acetic acid‑sodium acetate buffer (pH=4.5, 0.2M) solution. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and current time curve (i-t) tests were recorded on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system at room temperature. The sample to be tested was dropped on a GCE having a diameter of 4mm to prepare a working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode was used as reference electrode and a platinum electrode as the counter electrode. The potential window of the CV test was set in the range of −0.5–1.0V, and the working voltage for i-t test was 0.6V. In addition, the substrate (catechol) was added into the electrolyte under continuous stirring slowly during the test.

The morphology of the F,N-CDs is observed by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the F,N-CDs exhibit quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of 1.68nm. The HRTEM image of an individual F,N-CD indicates that it has a well-resolved lattice fringes with an inter-planar spacing of 0.19nm, which is in agreement with the (100) facet of graphitic carbon core. Fig. 1C shows the TEM images of Lac-F,N-CDs complex, demonstrating that the F,N-CDs are uniformly attached to the skeleton of laccase. Fig. 1D–I shows fluorescence microscopy images of F,N-CDs (D, E, F) and Lac-F,N-CDs complexes (G, H, I) in different channels. Photographs in brightfield are shown in Fig. 1S. It can be observed that both F,N-CDs and Lac-F,N-CDs complex are fluorescent and the latter's fluorescence is stronger, further indicating the F,N-CDs are attached to the laccase.

The chemical compositions of F,N-CDs were investigated by XPS, FT-IR and NMR. Fig. 2A shows the survey scan of the XPS spectrum. The surface atomic concentrations of C, N, O and F are 63.16%, 15.90%, 18.69% and 2.26%, respectively. Besides, the high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) shows four peaks at 284.71 [41], 285.48 [42], 287.28 [43–44] and 288.98eV (XPS standard energy spectrum), which can be assigned to CC/CN, CN/CO, CF and COOH, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.67 and 400.67eV, respectively. One peak located at 399.67eV [45] corresponds to pyrrolic nitrogen, which should contribute to sp 2-hybridized, π-conjugated system of carbon core, the other peak at 400.67eV [45] can be attributed to graphitic nitrogen, which means some of the carbon atoms in the sp
2 -hybridized carbon core are replaced by nitrogen atoms. As previously reported, the incorporated 5 atomic % nitrogen in N-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes could boost the electrical current generated by the oxidation of the analyte [8]. In our present work, a much higher concentration of N-doping (15.90 atomic %) in F,N-CDs can certainly improve the electronic transport and thus enlarge the electrochemical detection signal. In addition, the low intensity ratio of I
D
/I
G (0.34) in Raman spectrum demonstrates the high crystallinity of graphitic F,N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 2S [35]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3S) reveals the presence of CO bonds (531.78eV) and CO bonds (532.69eV) [42]. The F1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) exhibits two different kinds of bonding configurations that are 685.67eV and 687.91eV due to the semi-ionic CF bonding and the covalent CF bonding, respectively [6,46,47]. Moreover, the FI-IR spectrum (Fig. 4S) indicates the presence of CN bond (2106cm−1), CC/CO bond (1680cm−1), CF bond (1401cm−1) and fatty amine stretching vibration (1179cm−1) in the sample of F,N-CDs. Further, the 19F NMR spectrum (Fig. 5S) confirms the presence of two different kinds of CF bonds with the chemical shift values of −143 and −111ppm. It can be elucidated as F- combined with the edge of F,N-CD and F-linked to uracil heterocyclic, which corresponds to the semi-ionic CF and the covalent CF bonding in the XPS data, respectively.

The optical properties of the F,N-CDs are also fascinating. Fig. 3A shows UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of F,N-CDs. There are two absorption peaks at 235nm and 267nm, which can be attributed to π-π* and n-π* electron transitions, respectively [48]. Besides, it can be seen an emission peak at 523nm under the excitation of 373nm in the PL spectrum. F,N-CDs have a high fluorescence quantum yield of 45.6% (relative) and 9% (absolute) in water. The inset of Fig. 3A shows that the aqueous solution containing F,N-CDs is transparent under visible light, which indicates that the F,N-CDs can disperse well in water. According to the previously reported method [49], the hydrophilic-lipophilic-balance (HLB) value of the F,N-CDs is evaluated by means of 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 6S). The HLB value of the F,N-CDs is 16.22, which suggests that the F,N-CDs are highly hydrophilic probably arising from the co-existence of N- and F-containing surface groups and thus well soluble in water. In fact, there are previous reports on hydrothermal synthesis of water-dispersible CDs using p-PD as a starting material [41,50]. Additionally, we have measured the average size of the F,N-CDs in aqueous solution by using a visual multispectral particle analyzer, the result shows that the average size of the F,N-CDs in aqueous solution is 173nm and the size distribution is relatively narrow (Fig. 7S). All of these results have demonstrated that the as-prepared F,N-CDs present good water solubility, which is helpful to form complex with laccase in aqueous solution in the next step.

A series of F,N-CDs aqueous solution were prepared with different concentrations of 0, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 200 and 250μgmL−1, and then each solution was added to a 500μgmL−1 laccase solution. Fig. 3B shows that the UV–Vis absorption peak blue shifts as the F,N-CDs concentration increases, although the intensity of the fluorescence decreases (Fig. 8S). Moreover, a new shoulder peak at about 471nm appears in the PL spectrum (Fig. 3C) when 125μgmL−1 F,N-CDs aqueous solution is added to the laccase solution，and the fluorescence intensity of F,N-CDs decreased after it combined with laccase, which further indicated that electron or energy transfer occurs between F,N-CDs and laccase [51]. Both UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra suggest the formation of a sort of Lac-F,N-CDs complex. Further, as shown in Fig. 3D, fluorescence responses of F,N-CDs to various kinds of metal ions reveal a high selectivity of F,N-CDs towards Cu2+ and Cu+ ions, which provides an evidence of the formation of Lac-F,N-CDs complex through F,N-CDs coordinating with copper centers (T1, T2, T3) of laccase.

The CV responses of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE to catechol (1.0mM) were recorded at different scan rates (10, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200 and 250mVs−1), as shown in Fig. 4A. It is obvious that the oxidation and reduction peaks shift oppositely to the higher and lower potential regions while increasing the sweep speeds, accompanied with enhancement of redox peak currents. At the maximum scan rate of 250mVs−1, an oxidation peak at around 0.6V appears with the largest current intensity. Fig. 4B shows plots of the oxidation and reduction peak currents against the square roots of the sweep speeds, both indicate a good linear relationship, which means that the electrochemical reaction on the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE electrode is a diffusion-controlled electron transfer process [52]. More prominently, the as-prepared Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE electrode exhibits an excellent cycling stability. As shown in Fig. 4C, the peak current of the CV after 50 times cycle has dropped very slightly (5.57%), and the peak area integral is scarcely decreased (2.31%), too. Further, cycling stability of the same Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE electrode was examined after the storage of 30days at 4°C. As displayed in Fig. 9S, the CV curves remain their shapes as well as redox peak positions during the overall 50 times cycle, although the oxidation and reduction peak currents have reduced to one-eighth and one-sixth of their original values (Fig. 4C). In the present experiment, the remarkable 50 times cycling stability reveals that the Lac-F,N-CDs complex does not disassociate and therefore the laccase will not detach from the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE electrode easily. Nevertheless, CV has proven to be very useful for initial electrochemical studies of new systems to obtain information about electrode reactions [53]. Fig. 4D shows the CV curves obtained using different electrodes: bare GCE, F,N-CDs/GCE and Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE in absence (−) and presence (+) of catechol. No redox peak was observed from CVs for the bare GCE, F,N-CDs/GCE and Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE in the absence (−) catechol. The voltammogram for bare GCE and F,N-CDs/GCE shows poorly defined peaks, whereas the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE shows a well-defined redox peaks, owing to the direct electron transfer of the active center of the laccase and the electrode surface [13].

The electrochemical analysis of the designed electrochemical biosensor (Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE) was obtained on basis of amperometric i-t responses using continuous additions of catechol, as shown in Fig. 5A. The corresponding enlarged region (Fig. 5B) shows that the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE electrode has fast response time and a relatively good stability. Fig. 5C shows the calibration plot of the current densities vs. catechol concentrations. When the signal-to-noise ratio is 3 (S/N=3), a linear regression equation can be obtained as Eq. (2) with the coefficient (R 2) of 0.9997 indicating a good quality of fit [1], where J is the current density. As a result, a high sensitivity of 219.17μAcm−2 mΜ−1 is achieved and thereafter a detection limit of 0.014μM can be estimated from reported literature [12]. The reproducibility and repeatability of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor were examined under the same conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for electrochemical sensor reproducibility is 11.76%, as shown in Fig. 10S.
(2)
J
μA
cm
− 2
= 219.17
C Catechol
mM
− 0.1199

Effect of pH on the detection of catechol by Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE was explored in range of 1.0–12.0. As shown in Fig. 11SA, the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor is stable under acidic conditions and its oxidation peak current is reduced by 60% from the maximum at a pH of 12. Besides, the relationship between the oxidation peak potential and pH was studied (Fig. 11SB), and the linear fit equation for the pH of solution and its peak potential is expressed in Eq. (3). The slope of this equation is close to the theoretical value of 59mV pH − 1 for the two-electron and two-proton processes [54], indicating that the redox reaction of catechol at Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE should be a two-proton and two-electron process.
(3)
E
mv
= − 56.35 pH + 714.44
R 2
= 0.9794

Further, we have tested the sensor selectivity in the presence of various phenolic compounds. Fig. 5D shows the catalytic currents of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE towards different substrates with the same concentrations (1.0mM) of 1-catechol and interferences including 2-glucose, 3-hydroquinone, 4-dopamine, 5-gallic acid, 6-phenol and 7-p-nitrophenol. In addition, we have also investigated the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor's selectivity with the interferences concentration 10 times more than catechol concentration, the result is shown in Fig. 5E. There are obvious i-t responses can be seen to successive additions of catechol, hydroquinone, dopamine and gallic acid, but showing no response to the additions of glucose, phenol and p-nitrophenol. This result has demonstrated that glucose, phenol and p-nitrophenol have no obvious interference to catechol detection while using the sensor of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE. Moreover, we have used cyclic voltammetry to detect samples with mixture of various phenols. As shown in Fig. 6A, the Lac-FN-CDs/GCE displays different redox potentials in CV curves while response to catechol, hydroquinone, dopamine and gallic acid, respectively. Also the shape of CV curve of gallic acid varies remarkably from those of catechol, hydroquinone and dopamine. Therefore, the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor can be employed to differentiate catechol from other polyphenols in samples by means of cyclic voltammetry.

In order to assess the practicality of the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor for detection of catechol, we through standard addition method to added a specific concentration of catechol in tap and lake water. The results analyzed by HPLC method obtained the recoveries are 108% to 137% (Table 1S). Hence, the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor has the potential to analyze real samples. For comparison, we have listed some of the previous reports [9,11–15,55–59] on detection of catechol in Table 1 .Obviously, the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE electrode shows a comparatively low detection limit, which confirms that F,N-CDs and laccase are good partners for construction of electrochemical sensors. The possible reason is mainly relied on the fact that the doped F- and N-containing groups are readily to form complex with copper ions (T1, T2 and T3) of the laccase through coordination chemistry [35]. Furthermore, we have studied the electrochemical response of the sensor in the presence or absence of F,N-CDs. The resulting CV curves are shown in Fig. 6B. Compared with the Laccase/GCE (a sensor without the presence of F,N-CDs), the Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor shows obviously enlarged current signals in response to catechol at a scan rate of 100mVs−1. Besides, the redox potential range is also broaden from 0.10 to 0.53V to 0.05–0.60V. This result demonstrates that F,N-CDs might act as a bridge to accelerate electron transfer process while catechol being oxidized to o-benzodiquinone [1,8,60]. Additionally, we have investigated photocurrent response of Lac-F,N-CDs and Lac to catechol under visible light (λ =400–500nm) irradiation which is produced by a LED lamp (40W, Kessil). Fig. 12S shows photocurrent response curves of Lac-F,N-CDs and Lac in the presence (+) and absence (−) of catechol upon light on/off at an interval of 60s. Obviously, no photocurrent was generated in the absence of catechol for both Lac-F,N-CDs and Lac. However, much larger photocurrent response of Lac-F,N-CDs can be generated than that of Lac in the presence of catechol, which can be mainly attributed to large photocurrent of F,N-CDs generated under visible light irradiation because of the UV–Vis absorption of the F,N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3A. This result suggests that electrochemical sensing of Lac-F,N-CDs for detection of catechol could be performed under visible light irradiation so as to obtain enhanced current signals.

In summary, the F,N-CDs are easily prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. A novel electrochemical sensor was constructed on the basis of F,N-CDs and laccase. Compared with the traditional detection methods of catechol, the as-fabricated sensor has many advantages such as fast response, a low detection limit (0.014μM) and good cycle stability. More remarkably, the laccase exhibits excellent operational immutability in the Lac-F,N-CDs complex after multiple cycles (≥50) of catalytic reaction with catechol, which means the recycling use of Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE sensor under ambient conditions. Electrochemical sensors based on Lac-F,N-CDs/GCE have potential applications for detecting catechol content in tap water, lake water and etc., in which CDs play an important role in reducing limit of detection, increasing selectivity and cycle stability. However, due to the complexity of real samples, the potential of carbon nanomaterials for the analysis of more complex samples should be further explored.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was finance supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51772001), Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing, and Key Laboratory of Environmental-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Hefei, Anhui, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence microscope images, Raman, XPS, FT-IR, 19F- NMR, and 1H- NMR spectra of F,N-CDs (Fig. 1S-7S). PL intensity of different concentrations of F,N-CDs and laccase complex (Fig. 8S). CV curves of 50 times cycle, catechol, hydroquinone, dopamine and gallic acid (Fig. 9S-11S). Photocurrent response curves of Lac and Lac-F,N-CDs (Figure 12S). Determination of catechol in tap water and lake water using the proposed methods (n=3) (Table 1S).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2019.03.071.
